Why the Intelligence Age Will Reward Indispensable Regions
Kumamoto is not the subject of this essay. It is the lens through which a broader question is explored.
Introduction
For much of the twentieth century, economic development followed a familiar formula.
Regions sought a competitive advantage by attracting factories, reducing production costs, improving logistics, and expanding industrial clusters. Manufacturing excellence became the foundation of national prosperity.
That framework shaped the Industrial Age. The Intelligence Age is beginning to reshape it.
Artificial intelligence is transforming far more than software. It is changing how economic value is created, how innovation spreads, and how regions participate in global production networks.
Manufacturing remains essential. But manufacturing alone is no longer sufficient.
The defining strategic question is no longer:
Who can manufacture the most?
Instead, it is becoming:
Who can become indispensable?
Indispensability is fundamentally different from competitive advantage.
Competitive advantage is relative. It depends on outperforming competitors.
Indispensability is structural. It describes capabilities that the global system cannot easily replace.
This distinction matters because the Intelligence Age is increasingly organised around interconnected AI Value Networks rather than traditional supply chains.
Throughout this essay, I use the term AI Value Network to describe the interconnected system linking semiconductor design, manufacturing, AI models, cloud computing, robotics, software, deployment, and continuous learning. Value is created not by isolated firms or regions, but through collaboration across this network.
Within such networks, the most valuable regions are not necessarily those that manufacture the most products. They are the regions that become strategic nodes — places where knowledge, technology, talent, and deployment converge in ways that are difficult to replicate.
Viewed through this lens, Kumamoto is far more than the location of a new semiconductor factory. It offers an opportunity to explore a broader question facing every advanced economy:
How can regions build long-term indispensability in the Intelligence Age?
Why TSMC Matters
The arrival of TSMC in Kumamoto is often described — accurately, but incompletely — as a manufacturing success story. Its deeper significance lies in the network it connects Kumamoto to.
TSMC is not simply one of the world’s leading semiconductor manufacturers. It occupies a uniquely important position within the global AI Value Network. Its advanced manufacturing capabilities support companies such as NVIDIA, Apple, AMD, Broadcom, and countless others whose technologies increasingly define the digital economy.
This shift is already visible in national policy. In November 2024, Japan’s Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry approved a Framework for Strengthening the AI and Semiconductor Industrial Infrastructure, committing public support of more than ¥10 trillion through FY2030 to catalyse over ¥50 trillion in combined public-private investment over the next decade — an effort the Ministry projects will generate an economic ripple effect of roughly ¥160 trillion. TSMC’s Kumamoto investment sits inside this broader architecture, not apart from it.
In the Intelligence Age, competitive advantage is rarely created by individual organisations acting alone. It emerges through networks of complementary capabilities.
The future of Kumamoto therefore, depends not simply on hosting advanced manufacturing facilities. Its greater opportunity lies in becoming a strategic node where manufacturing excellence, research institutions, engineering talent, entrepreneurship, and AI applications reinforce one another.
The factory is only the beginning. The ecosystem — and the capabilities it generates — will determine whether Kumamoto becomes genuinely indispensable.

Source: Committee on New Direction of Economic and Industrial Policies, Industrial Structure Council, Ministry of Economy, Trade and Industry, “Fourth Report of the Committee on New Direction of Economic and Industrial Policies — Industrial Structure in 2040 Led by Growth Investment” (June 3, 2025), pp. 4, 39–40.
About the Author
Kenji Kimura is an independent analyst based in Kumamoto, Japan. Drawing on experience in corporate strategy, organizational development, and global workforce consulting, he writes about AI strategy, semiconductor ecosystems, industrial policy, and regional innovation — exploring how emerging technologies reshape long-term criticality and centrality for companies, regions, and nations.
